title: Mapping urban green space across Africa
markdown:
By **Jorge Patiño**, _Sahel and West Africa Club, OECD_, [@cocovski] (https://twitter.com/cocovski), 2024

**_Topic:**_ Climate, Urbanisation, Sustainability 

**_Keywords:**_ Africa; Urban green spaces; Sustainability; Resilience; Nature-based solutions; Ecosystem services

**_Africapolis data_**: GIS, agglomeration data


_***Original article:**_ Anderson, B., J.E. Patiño Quinchia, R. Prieto-Curiel (2022), Boosting African cities’ resilience to
climate change: The role of green spaces. West African Papers, No. 37, OECD Publishing, Paris. https://doi.org/10.1787/3303cfb3-en 

___

**_Abstract:**_

_Rapid urbanisation and climate stress are significantly impacting African cities and will continue to exacerbate these challenges in the future. Green spaces play a crucial role in enhancing resilience to heatwaves, floods, and other climate-related risks while improving air quality, protecting biodiversity, absorbing carbon, and increasing well-being. Despite their importance, data on green spaces in African urban areas is limited. This work addresses this gap by estimating green space availability in 5,625 urban agglomerations across Africa, exploring their dynamics with urbanisation and potential to bolster resilience and sustainability. Effective use of green spaces hinges on their strategic placement within urban layouts. Local support combined with national and regional backing is essential to fully utilise their benefits._ 

Africa is undergoing rapid urbanisation; projections indicate an additional 950 million people in cities by 2050 compared to 2015. This growth coincides with escalating climate stress, disproportionately impacting the continent. African cities face multiple risks, including extreme heat, flash floods, droughts, sea-level rise, and intensified storms, threatening infrastructure and public safety. Effective responses to these challenges rely heavily on local initiatives to mitigate escalating urban risks. 

![img](/images/tempimages/Patino_UGS_0_EN.png)

######*Map of urban green space in Abuja (Nigeria).* 

Urban green spaces (defined here as areas covered by trees, shrubs, and grasslands, see map above showing these in Abuja, Nigeria) are important tools for cities to increase resilience to climate change. Beyond resilience, green spaces offer diverse ecosystem services that enhance sustainability and quality of life. These include mitigating air and noise pollution, sequestering carbon, improving water quality, and conserving biodiversity. These services have substantial public health implications, from reducing asthma mortality rates in children to alleviating heat stress among the elderly and fostering social cohesion and mental well-being. Political agendas increasingly recognise the importance of green spaces. The African Union's Green Action Recovery Plan 2021-27 and SDG 11's Target 7 underscore the push for universal access to public green spaces by 2030, highlighting their role in sustainable urban development.

Despite their benefits, comprehensive data on green spaces in African cities remains scarce, particularly for smaller and intermediate cities. Limited data impedes, for example, evaluating what happens to green spaces within cities  as they urbanise and their potential as nature-based solutions to climate change. To address this gap, this analysis integrates data from the European Space Agency’s [World Cover Map](https://esa-worldcover.org/en) and Africapolis, offering insights into green space availability across 5,625 urban agglomerations in Africa. This new dataset is accessible online through the [Mapping Territorial Transformations in Africa](https://mapping-africa-transformations.org/climate/) platform (MAPTA), as shown in the map below, to provide African stakeholders as well as development partners with valuable information to plan for climate change. 

![img](/images/tempimages/Patino_UGS_1_EN.png)

######*Map of urban green space cover in African cities. The size of the bubbles represents the city's population size, the color represents the percentage of the urban agglomeration covered by green spaces (explore the interactive map on [MAPTA](https://mapping-africa-transformations.org/climate/)).*

##### Managing trade-offs between compactness and green spaces in African cities

The analysis reveals greater compactness (i.e., less space between buildings) is associated with less green space availability in African cities (see figure below), which could exacerbate urban heat island effects. Taller buildings (multiple floors, not necessarily skyscrapers), a sign of vertical growth, are associated with greater green space availability compared to flatter skylines in African cities. Given this findings, ensuring the protection green spaces whilst urbanising requires more than urban planning provisions; it also demands sufficient resources, clear jurisdictional frameworks, and robust enforcement mechanisms. 

![img](/images/tempimages/Patino_UGS_2_EN.png)

######*Examples of bird-eye views of three different cities with similar population sizes. The circles are centered on the most built-up point in each city. Cities of similar sizes can have drastically different amounts of green spaces.*

###### More green space, better air quality?

Green spaces also improve air quality, the latter of which is of pressing concern as outdoor air pollution is increasing in Africa driven by the use of fossil fuels, higher levels of traffic congestion, and the prevalence of very old vehicles. By combining data on the green spaces cover (as percentage of the urban area) with [satellite-derived PM2.5 concentrations](https://sites.wustl.edu/acag/datasets/surface-pm2-5/), the analysis shows that increased green space correlates with significantly reduced air pollution levels (see figure below), which illustrates their potential to be integral components of comprehensive urban environmental policies. However, none of the cities analysed meet the WHO's clean air standards, underscoring the critical need for enhanced green space initiatives. 

![img](/images/tempimages/Patino_UGS_3_EN.png)

######*Urban green space cover and air pollution in Africa. Urban green space is the percentage of the area of the urban agglomeration covered with green spaces, particular matter concentrations are the spatial mean within the urban agglomeration area.*

##### Green spaces' cooling effect: proximity matters

Now it is widely recognized that proximity to green spaces  is a means to attenuate heatwave impacts, since temperatures can be 2-3°C lower in the areas close to them. We find though that availability of green spaces does not necessarily mean that people live in proximity, or that it is accessibile. For instance, cities like Abuja and Accra may have similar green space coverage (almost 20% of the urban footprint, as shown in the chart below), but drastically different proportions of the population can benefit from its cooling effects during a heat wave: 16% in Accra and 55% in Abuja. Local actors who plan to use green spaces as a nature-based solution to heat waves should try to ensure varying-sized patches well dispersed throughout the city. 

![img](/images/tempimages/Patino_UGS_4_EN.png)

######*Proximity to urban green spaces in a selection of African cities. Cities located at the same height in this chart have a similar percentage of green space cover. The horizontal axis represents the percentage of the population located close to green spaces with trees that can deliver a significant cooling effect (Explore an interactive visualization on [MAPTA](https://mapping-africa-transformations.org/climate/))*

Urban green spaces have great potential to increase cities’ sustainability and resilience. While the choices of local actors greatly influence how cities evolve, they often need support from national and regional governments. In many highly centralised countries, the ability of local-level actors to raise revenue and their financial capacity are significantly restricted, with limited transfers and centralised urban planning. This constrains their  ability to restore, maintain, and enforce existing regulations that pertain to green spaces. Additionally, many national governments do not fully recognise the importance of the local-level efforts in  mitigation and adaptation. Moving forward, empowering local-level actors and fostering multi-level governance frameworks are needed to realise the full potential of green spaces in African cities. 

*The views, conclusions and materials of external contributors do not represent the views of OECD/SWAC or its Members.*

####*Author’s biography* 

![img](/images/tempimages/Patino_bio_rect.png)

*[Jorge Patiño](mailto:jorge.patino@oecd.org) holds an M.Sc. in Earth Sciences and a Ph.D. in Remote Sensing and GIS. He leads the Territorial Transformations Lab at OECD/SWAC, where he focuses on leveraging open geospatial data to enhance climate resilience and sustainability, with particular emphasis on the role of urban green spaces.*
